message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Caly, Druce (1) reported that ivermectin inhibited SARS-CoV-2
                    <jats:italic>in vitr</jats:italic>
                    o for up to 48 h using ivermectin at 5μM. The concentration resulting in 50% inhibition (IC
                    <jats:sub>50,</jats:sub>
                    2 µM) was >35x higher than the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) after oral administration of the approved dose of ivermectin when given fasted.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Simulations were conducted using an available population pharmacokinetic model to predict total (bound and unbound) and unbound plasma concentration-time profiles after a single and repeat fasted administration of the approved dose of ivermectin (200 μg/kg), 60 mg, and 120 mg. Plasma total Cmax was determined and then multiplied by the lung:plasma ratio reported in cattle to predict the lung Cmax after administration of each single dose.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Plasma ivermectin concentrations of total (bound and unbound) and unbound concentrations do not reach the IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    , even for a dose level 10x higher than the approved dose. Even with higher exposure in lungs than plasma, ivermectin is unlikely to reach the IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    in lungs after single oral administration of the approved dose (predicted lung: 0.0857 µM) or at doses 10x higher that the approved dose administered orally (predicted lung: 0.817 µM).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The likelihood of a successful clinical trial using the approved dose of ivermectin is low. Combination therapy should be evaluated
                    <jats:italic>in vitro</jats:italic>
                    . Re-purposing drugs for use in COVID-19 treatment is an ideal strategy but is only feasible when product safety has been established and experiments of re-purposed drugs are conducted at clinically relevant concentrations.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The Approved Dose of Ivermectin Alone is not the Ideal Dose for the Treatment of COVID-19

reference.unstructured: Caly, L. , Druce, J. , Catton, M. , Jans, D. & Wagstaff, K. The FDA-approved Drug Ivermectin inhibits the replication of SARS-CoV-2 in vitro. Antivir Res Pre-proof, (2020).
STROMECTOL® (ivermectin) tablets [package insert]. Merck Sharp & Dohme Corp. <https://www.merck.com/product/usa/pi_circulars/s/stromectol/stromectol_pi.pdf> (Rev. 2018).
NCT02045069. Efficacy and Safety of Ivermectin Against Dengue Infection. (https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02045069, 2015).

(2018). Efficacy and Safety of Ivermectin against Dengue Infection: A Phase III, Randomized, Double-blind, Placebo-controlled Trial. The 34th Annual Meeting The Royal College of Physicians of Thailand.

reference.article-title: Population pharmacokinetics of oral ivermectin in venous plasma and dried blood spots in healthy volunteers

Patel, A. & Desai, S. Ivermectin in COVID-19 Related Critical Illness (April 6, 2020). Available at SSRN: https://ssrncom/abstract=3570270 or http://dxdoiorg/102139/ssrn3570270, (2020).

reference.article-title: Parenteral administration of ivermectin in a patient with disseminated strongyloidiasis
reference.journal-title: The American journal of tropical medicine and hygiene
